summary: I was born and raised in Lexington, Kentucky, before moving to Mount Sterling in 1971 with my husband. Lexington is where my roots are, and it shaped my early life before I got deeply involved in education and community work in other parts of Kentucky.

My husband and I moved to Mount Sterling in 1971, and that's where I really started my long career in education. I stayed involved in the community there for many years, eventually retiring in 2008 before taking on leadership roles in the local education foundation and then The Post Clinic.

Montgomery County is where The Post Clinic is based, and since 2011 I've been working there as the director. The clinic originally served Montgomery County and its contiguous counties, and it has grown significantly since then. I also worked with the Montgomery County Education Foundation before joining the clinic.

I have been with The Post Clinic since June 2011, and it has been the most rewarding experience of my life. The clinic started in 1996 serving Montgomery County and nearby counties, and now it serves nearly forty counties including parts of Virginia. We provide medical and dental care to people who often have no insurance or means to pay, and I handle the background work while doctors and nurses care for the patients. The clinic has grown from a cramped space in the old post office to a much larger facility gifted by Dr. Byram Ratliff and his wife Jennifer. The sense of community and the gratitude from patients make this work truly meaningful.

At one point, The Post Clinic did not accept Medicare patients because Medicare covers doctor visits and medications, but we made an exception for a very sick patient who only had Medicare Part A, which covers hospital visits. Eventually, the board changed the policy to accept Medicare patients who meet our income guidelines, which is important because some patients have limited coverage and still need our help.

We base our income eligibility at The Post Clinic on three-hundred percent of the Federal Poverty Level because that's what pharmaceutical companies require for their Patient Assistance Programs. This threshold helps us serve many patients who can't afford even generic medications, especially given rising costs of living like gas and groceries. It’s a critical benchmark that allows us to help those truly in need.

The Post Clinic serves patients all over Eastern Kentucky and beyond, including many counties throughout the state. I have lived and worked in Kentucky most of my life, starting in Lexington and Mount Sterling, and now serving a broad community through the clinic. Kentucky’s rural areas especially benefit from the medical and dental care we provide.

Menifee County was one of the original contiguous counties served by The Post Clinic when it started in 1996. Along with Montgomery, Bath, Powell, Clark, and Nicholas counties, Menifee County residents have had access to our services for many years.

Bath County is another one of the contiguous counties that The Post Clinic originally served starting in 1996. We have maintained strong outreach there as part of our mission to help those in need across the region.

Powell County was included among the first counties served by The Post Clinic when it began in 1996. Residents from Powell County have been able to come to the clinic for medical and dental care as part of our early outreach efforts.

Clark County was one of the five contiguous counties served by The Post Clinic at its inception in 1996. We have continued to serve patients from Clark County as the clinic expanded its reach throughout Eastern Kentucky.

Nicholas County was also part of the original group of contiguous counties served by The Post Clinic starting in 1996. The clinic’s mission has included helping people from Nicholas County who lack access to affordable healthcare.

In recent years, The Post Clinic has expanded beyond Kentucky and served patients from Virginia as well. Within the past six months, we have helped people from twenty-seven counties in Virginia, showing how far our outreach has grown.

Scott County is one of the many counties outside the original service area from which patients come to The Post Clinic. We have had patients from Scott County seeking medical and dental care, reflecting the broad reach of our services.

Mason County is another county from which patients have come to The Post Clinic. Our clinic serves a wide area, and Mason County residents are among those who benefit from our medical and dental programs.

We have even had patients come from Jefferson County, which is quite far from Montgomery County. This shows the great need for affordable healthcare across Kentucky and how The Post Clinic fills that gap for many people.

Pike County is one of the distant counties from which patients have traveled to The Post Clinic for care. We see a lot of need for dental care in particular from patients in Pike County and other far-flung areas.

Dr. Byram Ratliff and his wife Jennifer generously gave us the building we now occupy, which was a huge upgrade from our previous cramped space in the old post office. Their gift in 2017 allowed The Post Clinic to expand its services and provide better facilities for patients and staff.

Jennifer Ratliff, along with her husband Dr. Byram Ratliff, donated the building that The Post Clinic moved into in 2017. Their generosity greatly improved our capacity to serve patients and was a pivotal moment in the clinic’s growth.

Dr. Tingle joined The Post Clinic in 2017 and wanted to start a free dental program. His initiative led to the creation of the dental program after collaboration with other dentists like Dr. Roberts, expanding the clinic’s services beyond medical care.

Dr. Roberts worked with Dr. Tingle to help establish the free dental program at The Post Clinic. Their teamwork made it possible to offer dental care alongside medical services, which was a significant development for the clinic.

Dr. Razor is one of the dental professionals involved in The Post Clinic’s dental program. Along with Drs. Tingle and Roberts, Dr. Razor helps provide much-needed dental care to patients who otherwise could not afford it.
Matt Bevin was not mentioned in the transcript, so I do not have any personal experience or stories related to him.
The United States Congress was not discussed in my interview or experiences, so I don't have any direct connection or stories involving Congress.
